title: The Neural Forecaster: Mission 4's Honest Verdict
concept: Final Neural Forecasting Model and Honest Comparison (capstone synthesis)
learningObjectives: Combine architecture design, training, regularization, and sequence modeling into one final neural forecaster
Evaluate the neural forecaster using the exact same methodology (TimeSeriesSplit, metrics) as Mission 3's scikit-learn model, for a fair comparison
Report an honest verdict on whether the added complexity of a neural network was actually worth it for this specific problem
Identify what would need to change (more data, different architecture, different problem) for the verdict to flip
whyItMatters: This mission's entire premise was 'maybe a neural network can do better than scikit-learn' — and a mission that never actually answers that question honestly, one way or the other, has failed regardless of how sophisticated the model built along the way looks.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's honest neural-vs-classical verdict — whichever model wins, this becomes the actual forecasting component feeding Mission 5's optimisation work, chosen on evidence rather than on which mission felt more impressive to build.

problem.summary: A sophisticated neural forecaster has never been run through the same honest gauntlet as the sklearn model.
problem.content: You've built an increasingly sophisticated neural forecaster across this entire skill folder, but you've never actually run it through the same honest evaluation gauntlet — baseline comparison, TimeSeriesSplit, the same metrics — that Mission 3's scikit-learn model went through.

motivation.summary: Neural networks aren't automatically better; finding out honestly matters more than assuming it.
motivation.content: A fair fight matters here specifically because neural networks have a reputation for being 'better' that isn't automatically true — on a dataset this size, with these features, a simpler model might genuinely win, and finding that out honestly is more valuable than assuming the more complex model must be superior.

theory.summary: A fair comparison uses identical validation and asks whether the margin justifies the added complexity.
theory.content: A fair comparison requires identical evaluation conditions: the same TimeSeriesSplit folds, the same held-out test period, the same metric(s) chosen for the same reasons as in Mission 3's feature-importance-evaluation lesson, and the same naive baseline as the floor every candidate must beat. The 'honest verdict' isn't just a number — it's a specific claim: which model wins, by how much, and whether that margin is large enough to justify the neural network's added training time, complexity, and harder-to-interpret predictions compared to the scikit-learn baseline's simplicity and direct interpretability. If the neural model doesn't clearly win, the correct conclusion is to say so and explain why, not to keep tuning until it does.

visualExplanation.summary: Both models run through the identical evaluation pipeline, converging on one scoreboard.
visualExplanation.content: The scikit-learn model and the neural forecaster both run through the identical TimeSeriesSplit evaluation pipeline, converging on a single side-by-side scoreboard against the naive baseline.

interactiveDemo.summary: Review a live scoreboard and write the honest verdict.
interactiveDemo.content: Review a live side-by-side scoreboard of the sklearn baseline, the neural forecaster, and the naive floor across every cross-validation fold, and be prompted to write the honest verdict yourself.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: run both models through identical evaluation and compare.
guidedCoding.content:
```python
sklearn_scores = cross_val_score(
    best_sklearn_model, X, y,
    cv=TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5),
    scoring="neg_mean_absolute_error",
)
neural_scores = evaluate_neural_model_cv(lstm_model, sequences, targets, n_splits=5)

print(f"Naive baseline MAE: {baseline_mae}")
print(f"Best sklearn model MAE: {-sklearn_scores.mean():.2f}")
print(f"Neural forecaster MAE: {neural_scores.mean():.2f}")
```

independentCoding.summary: Compute the neural model's percentage improvement over both baselines.
independentCoding.content: Compute the percentage improvement (or regression) of the neural forecaster over the sklearn baseline, and over the naive baseline, expressed as two separate numbers.

debugging.summary: Fix a comparison where the two models used unequal amounts of historical context.
debugging.content: The neural model's sequences use a 24-hour window while the sklearn model's features only look back 1 hour, meaning the two models were never actually competing on equal information. Identify the mismatch and align the comparison fairly.

reflection.summary: Winning due to more context, not architecture, is a different and important finding.
reflection.content: If the neural model won only because it had access to more historical context, not because it's inherently a better architecture for this problem, what would that actually tell you about where the real improvement came from?

teachBack.summary: Deliver the honest classical-vs-neural verdict and state what would flip it.
teachBack.content: As the synthesis of Mission 3 and 4 combined: deliver the honest verdict on classical ML vs. neural networks for demand forecasting on this dataset, and state specifically what would need to change for that verdict to flip.

miniProject.summary: Produce the final Mission 4 deliverable: a fair, honest, written comparison.
miniProject.content: Produce the Digital Twin's final Mission 4 deliverable: a side-by-side, fairly-evaluated comparison of the naive baseline, the best Mission 3 scikit-learn model, and the Mission 4 neural forecaster, with a written, honest verdict and a chosen model to carry forward into Mission 5.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This honest verdict determines which forecasting component feeds Mission 5.
portfolioEvidence.content: This honest verdict — not an assumption that 'neural is better' — determines which forecasting component actually feeds Mission 5's battery and EV optimisation work.

labs.title: Honest Comparison
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise requiring a fair, identically-evaluated comparison of the naive baseline, the Mission 3 sklearn model, and the Mission 4 neural forecaster, including a planted unequal-context bug to fix.

simulations.title: sklearn vs. Neural Scoreboard
simulations.description: Live side-by-side scoreboard of the naive baseline, sklearn model, and neural forecaster across every cross-validation fold.

flashcards.front: Why is it unfair to compare a neural model using 24 hours of context against an sklearn model using only 1 hour?
flashcards.back: Any performance difference could come purely from the amount of historical information available, not from which model architecture is actually better — a fair comparison requires giving both models equal access to context.

flashcards.front: What should the correct conclusion be if the neural model doesn't clearly beat the sklearn baseline?
flashcards.back: Report that honestly and explain why, rather than continuing to tune the neural model until it eventually 'wins' — the goal is an honest answer, not a predetermined one.

rubric: Both models are evaluated with identical validation methodology, metrics, and equal access to historical context
The written verdict states a specific winner (or a tie) with a quantified margin, not a vague impression
Limitations/what-would-flip-the-verdict discussion is specific and evidence-based, not speculative filler
